meta.name: System Call Tracer (strace clone)
meta.description: ptrace-based syscall interception and decoding for x86_64 Linux
meta.essence: Process introspection through kernel-mediated interception of system calls on x86_64 Linux, requiring register manipulation, word-by-word argument decoding from tracee memory, and process state management across fork/exec boundaries.
meta.why_important: Understanding ptrace-based tracing reveals how debuggers, profilers, and security tools interact with the kernel to observe program behavior — foundational knowledge for systems programming and tooling development.
learning_outcomes: Implement ptrace-based process attachment and syscall interception using PTRACE_SYSCALL
Track syscall entry/exit state to correctly read arguments vs return values
Decode syscall arguments by reading registers and dereferencing pointers from tracee memory word-by-word
Handle multi-process tracing across fork and exec with PTRACE_O_TRACEFORK
Build syscall filtering and timing statistics
Handle signal delivery to traced processes correctly

milestones.name: Basic ptrace Syscall Intercept
milestones.description: Use ptrace to trace a child process, stopping at each syscall entry and exit. Target architecture: x86_64 Linux.
acceptance_criteria: Fork a child process; child calls PTRACE_TRACEME then exec's the target program
Parent uses PTRACE_SYSCALL + waitpid loop to stop the child at each syscall boundary
Explicitly track entry/exit state with a toggle flag: on entry, read syscall number from orig_rax register; on exit, read return value from rax register
Print syscall number and return value for each intercepted syscall (e.g., "syscall(0) = 5")
Detect error returns: on x86_64, return values in range [-4096, -1] indicate error; display as -1 ERRNO (e.g., -1 ENOENT")"
Handle signals delivered to the tracee: when waitpid reports a signal-delivery stop (not a syscall stop), re-inject the signal using PTRACE_SYSCALL with the signal number, do not suppress it
Handle tracee exit cleanly: detect WIFEXITED/WIFSIGNALED from waitpid and exit the tracer
pitfalls: ptrace stops twice per syscall (entry and exit); failing to toggle state causes argument reads on exit and return-value reads on entry, producing garbage output
Register layout is x86_64-specific: orig_rax for syscall number, rax for return value; on i386 it's orig_eax/eax, on ARM it's completely different
PTRACE_TRACEME must be called in the child BEFORE exec(); after exec the tracee stops automatically on the first signal (SIGTRAP)
Signal stops and syscall stops are both reported via waitpid; must distinguish them using PTRACE_O_TRACESYSGOOD (sets bit 7 of signal in status) or by checking the signal number
Suppressing signals intended for the tracee (by passing 0 to PTRACE_SYSCALL instead of the signal number) breaks the traced program
concepts: ptrace system call and tracing lifecycle
deliverables: Child process creation with PTRACE_TRACEME + exec
PTRACE_SYSCALL + waitpid interception loop
Entry/exit toggle with correct register reads
Error return detection with errno mapping
Signal re-injection for non-syscall stops

milestones.name: Argument Decoding
milestones.description: Map syscall numbers to names and decode arguments including strings read word-by-word from tracee memory.
acceptance_criteria: Map x86_64 syscall numbers to human-readable names using a syscall table (at least the 50 most common syscalls)
Decode integer arguments from x86_64 argument registers: rdi, rsi, rdx, r10, r8, r9 (in that order)
Read string arguments (e.g., file paths for open/stat) from tracee memory using PTRACE_PEEKDATA in a word-by-word loop until a null terminator byte is found within a read word
Handle PTRACE_PEEKDATA correctly: set errno to 0 before call, check errno after (since -1 is both a valid data value and an error indicator)
Truncate displayed strings at a configurable maximum length (default 32 bytes) with "..." suffix
Format output matching strace style: syscall_name(arg1, arg2, ...) = return_value (e.g., 'open(/etc/passwd", O_RDONLY) = 3')"
Decode flag arguments for common syscalls: open() flags (O_RDONLY, O_WRONLY, O_CREAT), mmap() prot and flags
pitfalls: PTRACE_PEEKDATA reads one word (8 bytes on x86_64) at a time; string reading requires looping and scanning each word for a null byte, handling the case where the null is in the middle of a word
PTRACE_PEEKDATA returns -1 on error but -1 (0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF) is also valid data; must pre-clear errno and check it to distinguish error from data
Some syscalls have different argument semantics depending on flags (e.g., clone's arguments vary by flag bits); decode common cases, skip exotic ones
String pointers may be NULL (e.g., execve with NULL envp); must check for NULL before attempting PEEKDATA
Buffer overread: a string in the tracee might not be null-terminated within readable memory; set a maximum read length to prevent infinite loops
concepts: Word-by-word string extraction with null terminator scan
skills: Remote memory access across address spaces
Syscall ABI knowledge and argument interpretation
deliverables: Syscall number-to-name mapping table for x86_64
Register-based argument extraction per syscall signature
Word-by-word string reader with null terminator detection and length limit
Flag decoder for common syscalls (open, mmap, etc.)
strace-style formatted output

milestones.name: Multi-Process and Fork Following
milestones.description: Handle traced processes that fork or exec, tracing all descendants.
acceptance_criteria: Set PTRACE_O_TRACEFORK, PTRACE_O_TRACEVFORK, and PTRACE_O_TRACECLONE options on the tracee so child processes are automatically traced
Use waitpid(-1, ...) to catch events from ANY traced process (not just the original)
Detect fork/vfork/clone events via PTRACE_EVENT_FORK/VFORK/CLONE and retrieve new child PID with PTRACE_GETEVENTMSG
Tag all output lines with the PID of the process that made the syscall (e.g., "[pid 1234] open(...) = 3")
Handle PTRACE_EVENT_EXEC correctly: the tracee's memory map changes entirely after exec; reset any cached state for that PID
Handle multiple simultaneously traced processes without losing events or mixing up entry/exit state between PIDs
Maintain per-PID state tracking (entry/exit toggle, current syscall number) in a hash map
pitfalls: Must set PTRACE_O_TRACEFORK BEFORE the tracee calls fork, otherwise the child runs untraced and may exit before we can attach
waitpid(-1) returns events from any traced child; must dispatch to the correct per-PID state based on the returned PID
PTRACE_EVENT_EXEC stops look like SIGTRAP stops; must use status>>16 to extract the event type
After exec, the tracee has a new memory map; any cached memory pointers or string addresses from before exec are invalid
A traced child that forks creates a grandchild that must also be traced; this requires the PTRACE_O_TRACE* options to be inherited
skills: Per-process state tracking with hash maps
deliverables: PTRACE_O_TRACE* option setup for automatic child tracing
waitpid(-1) loop handling events from all traced processes
Per-PID state map tracking entry/exit toggle and current syscall
PID-tagged output for multi-process traces
Correct exec handling resetting per-PID cached state

milestones.name: Filtering and Statistics
milestones.description: Add syscall filtering by name and generate timing/frequency statistics.
acceptance_criteria: Filter output to show only specified syscalls by name (e.g., -e trace=open,read,write); unmatched syscalls are still traced (for dependency tracking) but not printed
Collect per-syscall wall-clock timing using clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC): record time at entry, compute duration at exit
Generate summary table (like strace -c) showing: syscall name, call count, error count, cumulative time, percentage of total time; sorted by cumulative time descending
Support -o filename flag to redirect trace output to a file instead of stderr
Support -p PID flag to attach to an already-running process using PTRACE_ATTACH (instead of fork+exec)
Detach cleanly from attached process on tracer exit or SIGINT using PTRACE_DETACH
pitfalls: Timing must use CLOCK_MONOTONIC not CLOCK_REALTIME; CLOCK_REALTIME is subject to NTP adjustments that can produce negative durations
Error detection requires checking if the x86_64 return value is in [-4096, -1]; simply checking < 0 misclassifies legitimate negative return values
PTRACE_ATTACH sends SIGSTOP to the target; must waitpid for the SIGSTOP before beginning PTRACE_SYSCALL tracing
File output with -o must handle concurrent writes from multiple traced processes; use per-line write() calls or a mutex
Signal filter: when attached to a running process, existing signal handlers must not be disrupted; always re-inject signals
deliverables: Syscall name filter with -e trace=name,name syntax
Per-syscall timing with CLOCK_MONOTONIC
Summary statistics table (count, errors, time, percentage)
File output option with -o flag
PTRACE_ATTACH support for tracing running processes with -p PID
Clean detach on exit/signal

blueprint.title: System Call Tracer (strace clone)
blueprint.overview:
This project builds a fully functional strace clone from scratch — a ptrace-based syscall interception and decoding tool for x86_64 Linux. You will implement the entire tracing lifecycle: forking a child process, intercepting every system call at entry and exit, decoding arguments by reading registers and remote memory word-by-word, following fork/exec across process boundaries, and generating timing statistics.

The project progressively reveals how the kernel mediates all observation of running processes. Every debugger, profiler, sandbox, and security tracer you've ever used relies on the same ptrace machinery you'll implement here. By building strace, you learn not just the ptrace API, but the fundamental x86_64 syscall ABI, the subtleties of process state management, and why observing a system always perturbs it.

By the end, you'll have a tool that can attach to any running process, decode its syscalls with human-readable arguments and flags, follow all child processes, filter by syscall name, and produce a statistical summary — a genuine systems debugging instrument.
blueprint.design_philosophy: This project teaches by forcing you to negotiate with three layers simultaneously: the application layer (your tracer's logic), the kernel layer (ptrace stops, waitpid semantics, signal delivery), and the hardware layer (x86_64 register conventions, word-aligned memory reads, cache-line-sized PEEKDATA operations). Every milestone shatters a comfortable abstraction — you discover that syscalls stop twice, that -1 is both valid data and an error, that observing a process changes its signal behavior. These are not edge cases; they are the core truths of process introspection that every systems programmer must internalize.

implementation.rationale: ptrace is a C-native Linux API. The register structures (struct user_regs_struct), PEEKDATA word semantics, errno-based error detection, and signal manipulation are all designed around C conventions. Using C eliminates the impedance mismatch between the API's mental model and the implementation language, and forces direct engagement with pointer arithmetic, word alignment, and manual memory management — exactly the skills this project teaches. Rust would add a safety layer that obscures the raw ptrace semantics; C keeps every byte visible.
implementation.style_guide: snake_case for functions and variables. UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for constants and macros. Structs use typedef with _t suffix (e.g., tracee_state_t). All functions return int for error codes unless they return data. Use explicit sizeof() everywhere. No implicit casts between pointer types. One .h/.c pair per logical module. Comments explain WHY, not WHAT.
implementation.build_system: Makefile with gcc, -Wall -Wextra -Werror -std=c11 -pedantic. Separate targets for debug (-g -O0 -fsanitize=address) and release (-O2). Test targets run against known programs with expected output diffs.
alternatives: Rust — viable with nix crate for ptrace, adds safety but obscures raw errno/PEEKDATA semantics that are core learning goals
Go — possible via syscall.PtraceSyscall but Go's runtime (goroutines, GC) complicates single-threaded tracer semantics and signal handling

assumed_known: C programming (pointers, structs, bitwise operations, malloc/free)
Basic Linux process model (fork, exec, waitpid)
What a system call is (user-space to kernel transition)
Basic signal concepts (SIGINT, SIGTERM, signal delivery)
Using man pages and header files

must_teach_first.when: Milestone 1 — must understand TRACEME → exec → SIGTRAP → PTRACE_SYSCALL loop before writing any code
Milestone 1 — must know orig_rax, rax, rdi, rsi, rdx, r10, r8, r9 and why orig_rax exists separately from rax
Milestone 1 — WIFEXITED, WIFSTOPPED, WSTOPSIG, WIFSIGNALED, WTERMSIG

must_teach_first.concept: errno semantics and the -1 ambiguity problem
must_teach_first.when: Milestone 2 — PTRACE_PEEKDATA returns -1 as valid data; must pre-clear errno
Milestone 2 — PEEKDATA reads 8-byte words, strings don't align to word boundaries

must_teach_first.concept: ptrace event stops vs signal stops
must_teach_first.when: Milestone 3 — status>>16 event extraction, PTRACE_O_TRACESYSGOOD bit 7

milestones.title: Basic ptrace Syscall Intercept
milestones.summary: Fork a child process that calls PTRACE_TRACEME and execs the target program. The parent enters a PTRACE_SYSCALL + waitpid loop, toggling between syscall entry and exit states. On entry, read the syscall number from orig_rax; on exit, read the return value from rax. Detect error returns using the x86_64 convention (return values in [-4096, -1]). Handle signal delivery stops by re-injecting signals, and detect tracee exit cleanly.
milestones.misconception: Developers assume ptrace stops the child once per syscall — you call PTRACE_SYSCALL, the child does a syscall, and you get notified with the result. It's a simple request-response.
milestones.reveal: ptrace stops the child TWICE per syscall — once at entry (before the kernel executes it) and once at exit (after the kernel returns). Both stops look identical from waitpid's perspective. If you don't maintain an explicit entry/exit toggle, you'll read the syscall number when you should read the return value, and vice versa, producing complete garbage. This double-stop design exists because debuggers need to inspect AND modify arguments before the kernel acts — it's the same mechanism that enables syscall sandboxing in seccomp-based tools like Docker's security profiles.
cascade: Syscall sandboxing (seccomp-bpf) — once you understand entry/exit interception, you see how container runtimes filter dangerous syscalls before the kernel executes them

Debugger breakpoints (cross-domain: software engineering tooling) — GDB uses the same ptrace stops to implement breakpoints, watchpoints, and single-stepping; the double-stop is why 'step over' and 'step into' can exist

x86_64 ABI register conventions — understanding orig_rax vs rax unlocks knowledge of how the kernel preserves syscall identity across the entry/exit boundary, which applies to any register-level debugging
Signal-stop vs syscall-stop distinction — this is the foundation for understanding all ptrace event types in Milestone 3
Observer effect in systems (cross-domain: physics/measurement) — tracing a process changes its timing, signal delivery, and scheduling; this is the Heisenberg principle of systems programming
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Fork a child process; child calls PTRACE_TRACEME then exec's the target program
Parent uses PTRACE_SYSCALL + waitpid loop to stop the child at each syscall boundary
Explicitly track entry/exit state with a toggle flag: on entry, read syscall number from orig_rax register; on exit, read return value from rax register
Print syscall number and return value for each intercepted syscall (e.g., "syscall(0) = 5")
Detect error returns: on x86_64, return values in range [-4096, -1] indicate error; display as -1 ERRNO (e.g., -1 ENOENT)
Handle signals delivered to the tracee: when waitpid reports a signal-delivery stop (not a syscall stop), re-inject the signal using PTRACE_SYSCALL with the signal number, do not suppress it
Handle tracee exit cleanly: detect WIFEXITED/WIFSIGNALED from waitpid and exit the tracer

milestones.title: Argument Decoding
milestones.summary: Map syscall numbers to human-readable names via a lookup table. Decode integer arguments from the x86_64 argument registers (rdi, rsi, rdx, r10, r8, r9). Read string arguments from tracee memory using PTRACE_PEEKDATA in a word-by-word loop, scanning each 8-byte word for a null terminator. Handle the PEEKDATA errno ambiguity. Decode bitmask flags for common syscalls. Format output in strace style.
milestones.misconception: Developers assume reading a string from another process is like reading from shared memory — just dereference the pointer. Or at worst, there's a 'read string from PID' system call that gives you the whole thing.
milestones.reveal: PTRACE_PEEKDATA can only read ONE WORD (8 bytes on x86_64) at a time. To read a string, you must loop, reading word after word, and scan each 8-byte chunk byte-by-byte for a null terminator. Worse, PTRACE_PEEKDATA returns -1 on error, but 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF is perfectly valid data (it's a common pointer value or bitmask). The only way to distinguish error from data is to clear errno to 0 before the call and check it afterward. This word-by-word reading exists because ptrace predates /proc/<pid>/mem and operates through the kernel's process isolation boundary — each PEEKDATA is a full context switch into kernel space, making it roughly 1000x slower than a local memcpy.
cascade: Process memory isolation — understanding why you can't just dereference a pointer across address spaces unlocks the concept of virtual memory and page tables; each process has its own address space mapping

/proc/<pid>/mem as alternative (cross-domain: Linux VFS) — modern tracers use this file-based interface for bulk reads, which is backed by the same page table walk but amortizes the syscall overhead; this is how Valgrind and sanitizers work

Word alignment and data marshalling — the byte-scanning-within-words pattern appears everywhere: network packet parsing, binary file format readers, and serialization libraries all deal with extracting variable-length data from fixed-width reads

Syscall ABI as a contract — the register ordering (rdi, rsi, rdx, r10, r8, r9) is the x86_64 System V ABI; understanding this contract unlocks knowledge of how compilers generate function calls, how FFI works between languages, and why r10 replaces rcx (the kernel uses rcx for the return address)

errno as global state (cross-domain: API design) — the PEEKDATA errno problem is a case study in why global mutable state creates bugs; modern APIs (Rust's Result<T,E>, Go's multiple returns) exist specifically to avoid this class of error
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Map x86_64 syscall numbers to human-readable names using a syscall table (at least the 50 most common syscalls)
Decode integer arguments from x86_64 argument registers: rdi, rsi, rdx, r10, r8, r9 (in that order)
Read string arguments (e.g., file paths for open/stat) from tracee memory using PTRACE_PEEKDATA in a word-by-word loop until a null terminator byte is found within a read word
Handle PTRACE_PEEKDATA correctly: set errno to 0 before call, check errno after (since -1 is both a valid data value and an error indicator)
Truncate displayed strings at a configurable maximum length (default 32 bytes) with "..." suffix
Format output matching strace style: syscall_name(arg1, arg2, ...) = return_value (e.g., open("/etc/passwd", O_RDONLY) = 3)
Decode flag arguments for common syscalls: open() flags (O_RDONLY, O_WRONLY, O_CREAT), mmap() prot and flags

milestones.title: Multi-Process and Fork Following
milestones.summary: Set PTRACE_O_TRACEFORK, PTRACE_O_TRACEVFORK, and PTRACE_O_TRACECLONE on the tracee so child processes are automatically traced. Use waitpid(-1, ...) to catch events from any traced process. Detect fork/vfork/clone events via PTRACE_EVENT extraction from the status word. Maintain a per-PID hash map tracking entry/exit toggle and current syscall. Handle PTRACE_EVENT_EXEC by resetting cached state. Tag all output with PIDs.
milestones.misconception: Developers assume that tracing a process automatically traces its children — after all, the child is created by the parent, so the tracer should 'inherit' observation. Or they assume fork() in a traced process creates a new, independent process that escapes observation.
milestones.reveal: Neither assumption is correct. By default, a forked child runs UNTRACED — it escapes immediately and may exit before you can attach. You must proactively set PTRACE_O_TRACEFORK *before* the tracee calls fork(). Once set, the kernel automatically ptrace-attaches the new child and stops it with a PTRACE_EVENT_FORK stop. But this creates a new challenge: your waitpid(-1) loop now receives interleaved events from multiple PIDs, each with independent entry/exit state. If you use a single global toggle, you'll immediately corrupt state when PID A is at entry and PID B is at exit. This is why strace maintains a per-PID state machine — and it's the same pattern used by container runtimes (Docker), process supervisors (systemd), and job schedulers (Slurm) that must track process trees.
cascade: Process trees and cgroups (cross-domain: containers/DevOps) — the fork-following pattern is exactly how Docker tracks all processes in a container; PTRACE_O_TRACECLONE catches thread creation via clone(), which is how the kernel implements both threads and processes

Per-entity state machines — maintaining independent state per PID is a pattern that appears in network servers (per-connection state), game engines (per-entity AI state), and database connection pools; the hash-map-of-state-machines pattern is universal
Event-driven architecture — the waitpid(-1) loop dispatching events to per-PID handlers is structurally identical to epoll-based event loops in web servers; understanding one deeply teaches the other

exec() memory map replacement — understanding that exec replaces the entire address space explains why ASLR works, why address sanitizer must be linked at program start, and why LD_PRELOAD injection happens before main()

Status word bit packing — the (status>>16) event extraction teaches how the kernel packs multiple pieces of information into a single integer, a pattern used in CPU flags registers, network packet headers, and file permission bits

Maintain per-PID state tracking (entry/exit toggle, current syscall number) in a hash map

milestones.title: Filtering and Statistics
milestones.summary: Add syscall filtering by name (-e trace=open,read,write) that suppresses output but continues tracing. Collect per-syscall wall-clock timing using CLOCK_MONOTONIC. Generate a summary statistics table showing call count, error count, cumulative time, and percentage — sorted by time descending. Support -o filename for file output, -p PID for attaching to running processes via PTRACE_ATTACH, and clean detachment on exit or SIGINT.
milestones.misconception: Developers assume that timing a syscall means measuring the time between your tracer's two stops — and that this gives you the syscall's actual cost. They also assume attaching to a running process is just like tracing from fork: call PTRACE_ATTACH and start the loop.
milestones.reveal: The time you measure between entry and exit stops includes significant tracer overhead — context switches into your tracer, ptrace kernel processing, and scheduler latency. A 'fast' syscall like getpid() takes ~100ns natively but may measure as 5-50μs under ptrace due to 2 context switches per syscall. Your statistics are useful for relative comparison but NOT absolute measurement. Furthermore, PTRACE_ATTACH sends a SIGSTOP to the target process, and you must waitpid() for this specific SIGSTOP before beginning PTRACE_SYSCALL — otherwise you'll race and miss the initial stop. And you might attach mid-syscall, meaning your first event is an EXIT, not an entry. CLOCK_MONOTONIC is mandatory because CLOCK_REALTIME can jump backward during NTP adjustments, producing negative durations. These three gotchas (observer overhead, attach sequencing, clock selection) are the same challenges faced by all profiling tools — from perf to DTrace to eBPF.
cascade: Profiling overhead and eBPF (cross-domain: observability) — ptrace's high overhead is exactly why eBPF was invented; eBPF runs filter programs in kernel space, avoiding context switches entirely; understanding ptrace's limitations explains why the industry moved to eBPF

CLOCK_MONOTONIC vs CLOCK_REALTIME — this distinction matters everywhere: database WAL timestamps, distributed system event ordering (Lamport clocks), animation frame timing in games, and CI pipeline duration measurement

Process attachment semantics — PTRACE_ATTACH's SIGSTOP + waitpid dance is the same pattern used by debuggers (gdb attach), crash reporters, and security scanners; the mid-syscall attach problem appears in any hot-attach scenario

Statistical aggregation patterns — the count/errors/time/percentage table you build is structurally identical to database query profilers (pg_stat_statements), HTTP request metrics (Prometheus histograms), and performance monitoring in any domain

Signal safety during detach (cross-domain: reliability engineering) — clean detach on SIGINT requires installing a signal handler in the tracer that calls PTRACE_DETACH for every tracked PID; this is the same 'cleanup on abnormal exit' pattern used in database connection pooling and file lock management
