Collect per-syscall wall-clock timing using clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC): record time at entry, compute duration at exit
Generate summary table (like strace -c) showing: syscall name, call count, error count, cumulative time, percentage of total time; sorted by cumulative time descending
Support -o filename flag to redirect trace output to a file instead of stderr
Support -p PID flag to attach to an already-running process using PTRACE_ATTACH (instead of fork+exec)
Detach cleanly from attached process on tracer exit or SIGINT using PTRACE_DETACH

diagrams.title: System Call Tracer — Satellite Map (Home Base)
diagrams.description: Project-wide architecture showing the tracer process, tracee process, kernel ptrace subsystem, and the four milestone zones. Every component has an anchor ID. Shows the three layers: tracer (user-space), kernel (ptrace + scheduler + signal delivery), and tracee (user-space target). Arrows show the ptrace control flow, waitpid notification flow, and PEEKDATA memory access paths. Each milestone zone is highlighted showing which components it adds or modifies.

diagrams.title: ptrace Tracing Lifecycle — Fork → TRACEME → Exec → SIGTRAP → Loop
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram showing the complete startup sequence: parent calls fork(), child calls ptrace(PTRACE_TRACEME) then execvp(), kernel sends SIGTRAP to child and stops it, parent's waitpid() returns, parent calls PTRACE_SYSCALL. Shows every syscall and signal exchange between parent, kernel, and child with exact timing relationships.

diagrams.title: Syscall Entry/Exit Double-Stop State Machine
diagrams.description: State machine diagram showing the toggle between ENTRY and EXIT states. On ENTRY: read orig_rax (syscall number), store it. On EXIT: read rax (return value), print line. Shows what goes wrong if you read the wrong register at the wrong stop — orig_rax at exit is stale, rax at entry contains garbage from the previous syscall's return. Includes a 'bug scenario' trace showing garbled output from a missing toggle.

diagrams.title: waitpid Status Word Bit Layout
diagrams.description: Bit-level structure_layout diagram of the waitpid status word (32-bit integer). Shows where WIFEXITED, WIFSTOPPED, WIFSIGNALED, WSTOPSIG, and WTERMSIG extract their information from. Highlights how PTRACE_O_TRACESYSGOOD sets bit 7 of the stop signal to distinguish syscall stops (SIGTRAP|0x80) from regular signal stops (SIGTRAP). Shows the status>>16 event extraction for PTRACE_EVENT_* in Milestone 3 as a preview.

diagrams.title: x86_64 struct user_regs_struct — Register Layout and Syscall ABI
diagrams.description: structure_layout diagram showing the complete struct user_regs_struct with byte offsets for every field. Highlights the syscall-relevant registers: orig_rax (syscall number, offset 120), rax (return value, offset 80), rdi (arg1, offset 112), rsi (arg2, offset 104), rdx (arg3, offset 96), r10 (arg4, offset 40), r8 (arg5, offset 72), r9 (arg6, offset 64). Shows WHY orig_rax exists separately: the kernel overwrites rax with -ENOSYS during entry, so the original syscall number must be preserved elsewhere.

diagrams.title: x86_64 Errno Detection: The [-4096, -1] Range
diagrams.description: Number line / before_after diagram showing the x86_64 return value space. Positive values and zero are success. The range [-4096, -1] (0xFFFFFFFFFFFFF001 to 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF in unsigned) maps to -errno values. Shows why simply checking < 0 is wrong: some syscalls legitimately return negative values (e.g., lseek can return large positive values that look negative in signed interpretation). Maps common errno values: -2 = ENOENT, -13 = EACCES, -22 = EINVAL.

diagrams.title: Signal Delivery in Traced Processes — Suppress vs Re-inject
diagrams.description: before_after diagram showing two scenarios: (1) WRONG: tracer calls ptrace(PTRACE_SYSCALL, pid, 0, 0) when a signal stop occurs — signal is suppressed, tracee never receives it, breaks signal-dependent programs. (2) CORRECT: tracer calls ptrace(PTRACE_SYSCALL, pid, 0, sig) — signal is delivered to tracee. Shows a real example: a program using SIGALRM for timeouts breaks under tracing if signals are suppressed.

diagrams.title: Three-Level View: Tracer → Kernel → Tracee Interaction
diagrams.description: Three-column interaction diagram showing what happens at each layer during a single syscall interception. Application layer: tracer calls ptrace(PTRACE_SYSCALL), blocks in waitpid(). Kernel layer: scheduler resumes tracee, tracee enters syscall, kernel checks ptrace flag, stops tracee, wakes tracer via waitpid. Hardware layer: context switch (TLB flush, register save/restore), syscall instruction (SYSCALL/SYSRET), register file read for PTRACE_GETREGS. Shows approximate cycle/nanosecond costs at each step.

diagrams.title: PTRACE_PEEKDATA Word-by-Word String Reading
diagrams.description: data_walk diagram showing how to read the string "/etc/passwd\0" from tracee memory. Address 0x7ffd1230 contains the string. PEEKDATA reads 8 bytes at 0x7ffd1230: bytes '/','e','t','c','/','p','a','s'. Next read at 0x7ffd1238: bytes 's','w','d','\0','<garbage>','<garbage>','<garbage>','<garbage>'. Shows the null-terminator scan within each word, the byte extraction using bit shifts and masks, and the assembly of the final string. Highlights word alignment requirement.

diagrams.title: The PEEKDATA -1 Ambiguity Problem
diagrams.description: trace_example diagram showing two scenarios: (1) PEEKDATA returns -1 (0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF) as VALID DATA (the tracee has this value at that address). errno remains 0. (2) PEEKDATA returns -1 as ERROR (invalid address). errno is set to EIO. Shows the WRONG approach (just check return == -1) and the CORRECT approach (clear errno to 0 before call, call PEEKDATA, check errno after). Includes C code for both approaches side-by-side.

diagrams.title: x86_64 Syscall Table — Number to Name to Argument Signature
diagrams.description: structure_layout / table diagram showing the syscall table structure. Each entry maps: syscall number → name → argument types and count. Shows 10-15 representative syscalls: read(0): fd, buf*, count; write(1): fd, buf*, count; open(2): path*, flags, mode; close(3): fd; stat(4): path*, statbuf*; mmap(9): addr*, length, prot, flags, fd, offset. Highlights which arguments are integers (read directly), which are pointers to strings (need PEEKDATA), and which are pointers to structs (need deep decoding).

diagrams.title: Bitmask Flag Decoding: open() Flags Example
diagrams.description: data_walk diagram showing how to decode the integer 0x242 from the flags argument of open(). Bit-by-bit breakdown: 0x200 = O_TRUNC, 0x40 = O_CREAT, 0x02 = O_RDWR. Shows the bitmask AND operation for each flag, the accumulation into the string "O_RDWR|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC", and edge cases: O_RDONLY is 0 (no bits set — must special-case), unknown bits should be printed as hex.

diagrams.title: Syscall Argument Register Mapping: From Signature to Registers
diagrams.description: Mapping diagram showing how a C function signature like open(const char *pathname, int flags, mode_t mode) maps to registers: pathname → rdi, flags → rsi, mode → rdx. Contrasts this with the user-space calling convention (which uses rcx for arg4) vs the syscall convention (which uses r10 for arg4, because SYSCALL instruction clobbers rcx with the return address). This is why r10 appears instead of rcx in the syscall ABI.

diagrams.title: Output Formatting Pipeline: Raw Registers → strace-style Line
diagrams.description: data_walk / trace_example showing the complete transformation pipeline for a single syscall. Input: orig_rax=2, rdi=0x7ffd3400, rsi=0x0, rdx=0x0, rax=3. Step 1: Lookup 2 → 'open'. Step 2: open takes (char*, int, mode_t) → decode arg1 as string via PEEKDATA → '/etc/passwd'. Step 3: decode arg2 as open flags → O_RDONLY. Step 4: return value 3 (positive = success). Output: open("/etc/passwd", O_RDONLY) = 3.

diagrams.title: Fork Following Event Sequence
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram showing what happens when a traced process calls fork(). (1) Tracee enters fork() syscall → entry stop in tracer. (2) Tracer continues with PTRACE_SYSCALL. (3) Kernel creates child, auto-attaches ptrace, stops child. (4) Kernel delivers PTRACE_EVENT_FORK stop to parent tracee. (5) Tracer's waitpid returns with parent PID and event status. (6) Tracer extracts child PID via PTRACE_GETEVENTMSG. (7) Tracer's next waitpid returns child PID with initial SIGSTOP. (8) Tracer adds child to PID state map, begins tracing both. Shows interleaving of events from both PIDs.

diagrams.title: Per-PID State Hash Map — Structure and Lookup
diagrams.description: structure_layout diagram showing the tracee_state_t struct: { pid_t pid; int in_syscall (0=entry_next, 1=exit_next); long current_syscall_nr; struct timespec entry_time; }. Shows the hash map with PIDs as keys and tracee_state_t as values. Illustrates a scenario with 3 PIDs (1000, 1001, 1002) at different states: PID 1000 awaiting exit for write(), PID 1001 awaiting entry, PID 1002 just forked and at initial stop. Memory layout with byte offsets for the struct.

diagrams.title: waitpid(-1) Event Dispatch Flowchart
diagrams.description: Flowchart diagram showing the complete event dispatch logic after waitpid(-1) returns. Branch 1: WIFEXITED → remove PID from map, if no PIDs left, exit tracer. Branch 2: WIFSIGNALED → same. Branch 3: WIFSTOPPED → check WSTOPSIG: if (SIGTRAP|0x80) → syscall stop → lookup PID in map → toggle entry/exit. If SIGTRAP and (status>>16) != 0 → event stop → decode PTRACE_EVENT_FORK/VFORK/CLONE/EXEC. If other signal → signal delivery stop → re-inject. Shows each branch leading to the correct PTRACE_SYSCALL call with appropriate signal argument.

diagrams.title: PTRACE_EVENT_EXEC: Memory Map Replacement
diagrams.description: before_after diagram showing a tracee's state before and after exec(). BEFORE: PID 1001, memory map has stack at 0x7ffd0000, heap at 0x5580000, loaded binary /usr/bin/bash, cached string pointer 0x7ffd3400 → '/tmp/data'. AFTER exec('/usr/bin/python3'): SAME PID 1001, entirely new memory map, stack at different address, different binary loaded, old pointer 0x7ffd3400 now points to unrelated data or unmapped memory. Shows why per-PID cached state must be reset: same PID, completely different process image.

diagrams.title: Multi-Process Trace Example: Shell Pipeline
diagrams.description: trace_example diagram showing what happens when you trace `sh -c 'cat /etc/passwd | grep root'`. Process tree: sh (PID 1000) forks PID 1001 (cat) and PID 1002 (grep), sets up pipe, each child execs. Shows the interleaved output with PID tags: [pid 1000] clone() = 1001, [pid 1000] clone() = 1002, [pid 1001] execve("/usr/bin/cat"...), [pid 1002] execve("/usr/bin/grep"...), [pid 1001] read(3, ...) = 1024, [pid 1001] write(1, ...) = 1024, [pid 1002] read(0, ...) = 1024. Demonstrates why per-PID state isolation is essential.

diagrams.title: Syscall Timing: What You Actually Measure vs True Cost
diagrams.description: before_after / trace_example diagram comparing: (A) Native syscall timing — getpid() takes ~100ns. (B) ptrace-traced timing — same getpid() measures 5-50μs because of: 2 context switches to tracer (each ~1-5μs), 2 PTRACE_SYSCALL calls, 2 waitpid returns, kernel ptrace bookkeeping. Shows the overhead breakdown as a stacked bar chart. Explains why ptrace statistics are useful for RELATIVE comparison but not ABSOLUTE measurement, and why eBPF/perf_events exist for low-overhead profiling.

diagrams.title: CLOCK_MONOTONIC vs CLOCK_REALTIME: Why the Wrong Clock Breaks Profiling
diagrams.description: state_evolution diagram showing two timelines. Timeline 1 (CLOCK_REALTIME): time flows normally, then NTP adjustment jumps clock backward by 200ms, syscall that took 1ms appears to have taken -199ms → negative duration → corrupt statistics. Timeline 2 (CLOCK_MONOTONIC): time always moves forward regardless of NTP; same syscall correctly measures 1ms. Shows the struct timespec layout and clock_gettime() call pattern.

diagrams.title: Syscall Filter Architecture: Trace Everything, Display Selectively
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram showing the filter pipeline. ALL syscalls are intercepted and timed (even unmatched ones — needed for per-PID state tracking and timing accuracy). A filter bitmask (one bit per syscall number, 512 bits = 64 bytes = 1 cache line) determines which syscalls produce output. Shows the -e trace=open,read,write parser converting names to numbers to bitmask bits. Explains why you can't skip unmatched syscalls at the ptrace level — you'd lose the entry/exit toggle sync.

diagrams.title: PTRACE_ATTACH Sequence: Attaching to a Running Process
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram showing: (1) Tracer calls ptrace(PTRACE_ATTACH, target_pid). (2) Kernel sends SIGSTOP to target. (3) Tracer MUST call waitpid(target_pid) and wait for SIGSTOP. (4) ONLY THEN can tracer call PTRACE_SYSCALL. Shows the race condition if you skip step 3: PTRACE_SYSCALL before SIGSTOP is received → ESRCH error. Also shows the mid-syscall attach problem: if target was inside a syscall when attached, the first stop is a syscall EXIT, not entry → toggle starts wrong → must handle gracefully.

diagrams.title: Summary Statistics Table Generation
diagrams.description: data_walk diagram showing how per-syscall statistics accumulate during a trace. For each syscall exit: increment count[syscall_nr], if error increment errors[syscall_nr], add duration to time[syscall_nr]. After trace completes: compute total_time = sum(all times), percentage = time[i]/total_time * 100, sort by time descending. Shows a sample output table matching strace -c format: % time, seconds, usecs/call, calls, errors, syscall — with real-looking numbers for a typical program.

diagrams.title: Clean Detach on SIGINT: The Cleanup Dance
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram showing the tracer's SIGINT handler. (1) User presses Ctrl+C → tracer receives SIGINT. (2) Signal handler sets global 'detaching' flag. (3) Main loop detects flag after next waitpid. (4) For each PID in state map: call ptrace(PTRACE_DETACH, pid, 0, 0). (5) If PID was stopped, DETACH resumes it. (6) Print statistics summary. (7) Exit tracer. Shows what goes wrong WITHOUT cleanup: tracee remains stopped forever (T state in ps), only killable with SIGKILL.

tdd_blueprint.design_vision: A ptrace-based syscall interception engine for x86_64 Linux that progressively reveals kernel-mediated process observation: from a single-process entry/exit toggle through full argument decoding via cross-address-space memory reads, to a multi-process event dispatcher with per-PID state machines, culminating in production-quality filtering, monotonic timing, and attach/detach lifecycle management. Every design decision is grounded in the hardware cost of context switching, TLB pressure, and the observer effect on measurement.

modules.name: Basic ptrace Syscall Intercept
modules.description: Implements the complete ptrace tracing lifecycle for a single child process: fork+PTRACE_TRACEME+exec setup, PTRACE_SYSCALL+waitpid event loop, entry/exit toggle state machine, x86_64 register reads (orig_rax/rax), error return detection via the [-4095,-1] range, and signal re-injection. Does NOT decode argument registers, does NOT follow fork/clone, does NOT implement filtering or statistics.

specs.inputs: argv[] of target program to exec; waitpid status words from kernel
specs.outputs: Line-per-syscall text to stderr: 'syscall(N) = RETVAL' or 'syscall(N) = -1 ERRNO (description)'
specs.abstractions: tracee_state_t {in_syscall: int, current_syscall_nr: long}; ptrace stop type classifier; errno_name() table; is_error_return() predicate
error_categories: PTRACE_TRACEME failure (child exits before exec)
execvp failure (program not found)
PTRACE_GETREGS failure (tracee died mid-stop)
waitpid EINTR (signal to tracer during wait)
Tracee exit via WIFEXITED or WIFSIGNALED
Signal-delivery stop requiring re-injection vs syscall stop
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded tracer; single tracee; sequential waitpid loop; no parallelism
performance_targets: Tracer loop latency: 2-10μs per ptrace stop (dominated by context switch cost)
PTRACE_GETREGS: single copy_to_user of 168 bytes (~50ns kernel work, ~1μs round-trip)
Steady-state: ~10x slowdown on traced program vs native (2 context switches per syscall)
Memory: tracee_state_t fits in single cache line (< 64 bytes)

implementation_phases.name: Fork/exec/PTRACE_TRACEME skeleton
waitpid loop with WIFEXITED/WIFSIGNALED/WIFSTOPPED dispatch
Entry/exit toggle + PTRACE_GETREGS + print syscall number and retval
Error return detection [-4095,-1] + errno_name table + signal re-injection

diagrams.title: ptrace Tracing Lifecycle: fork → PTRACE_TRACEME → exec → SIGTRAP → Loop
diagrams.description: Sequential lifecycle diagram showing the exact order of operations across parent (tracer) and child (tracee) from fork() through the steady-state PTRACE_SYSCALL+waitpid loop to WIFEXITED termination. Annotates which side executes each step, what kernel state transitions occur, and why PTRACE_TRACEME must precede exec.

diagrams.title: Syscall Entry/Exit Double-Stop State Machine
diagrams.description: State machine with two states (AWAIT_ENTRY, AWAIT_EXIT) and transitions triggered by each waitpid SIGTRAP stop. Labels each transition with: which register to read (orig_rax vs rax), what to print, what to save. Marks the ILLEGAL transition of reading rax at entry or orig_rax as return value at exit. Includes the initial state and the external WIFEXITED arc that exits the machine.

diagrams.title: waitpid Status Word Bit Layout
diagrams.description: Memory layout diagram of the 32-bit status integer returned by waitpid. Labels each bit field: bits[6:0] = termination signal or 0x7f sentinel, bit[7] = core dump flag, bits[15:8] = stop signal or exit code, bits[23:16] = PTRACE_EVENT_* value. Shows which POSIX macros decode which fields and the mutual exclusivity of WIFEXITED/WIFSIGNALED/WIFSTOPPED. Annotates the 0x80 syscall-stop bit from PTRACE_O_TRACESYSGOOD.

diagrams.title: x86_64 struct user_regs_struct: Register Layout and Syscall ABI Mapping
diagrams.description: Byte-offset memory layout of the full struct user_regs_struct (168 bytes). Highlights orig_rax offset vs rax offset with annotations explaining why both exist (rax is overwritten with return value; orig_rax preserves syscall number). Marks the six argument registers (rdi, rsi, rdx, r10, r8, r9) and explicitly flags r10 vs rcx as the critical difference from the C function-call ABI.

diagrams.title: waitpid Stop-Type Classification Decision Tree
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing the complete decision tree executed on every waitpid return: WIFEXITED → cleanup+break; WIFSIGNALED → cleanup+break; WIFSTOPPED → decode stop_sig → event!=0 branch vs sig==(SIGTRAP|0x80) branch vs plain SIGTRAP branch vs other-signal branch (re-inject). Each leaf shows the correct PTRACE_SYSCALL call and signal argument to pass.

diagrams.title: x86_64 Error Return Detection: The [-4095, -1] Range
diagrams.description: Number line visualization of the 64-bit signed integer space partitioned into: valid positive returns (0 to LONG_MAX), valid zero return, valid negative returns outside the error band, and the error band [-4095, -1] reserved for -errno. Shows concrete examples: read()=512 (valid positive), lseek()=-4096 (boundary case, valid data), open()=-2 (ENOENT, error). Contrasts with the naive retval<0 check and why it fails.

diagrams.title: Signal Re-injection vs Suppression: Tracer Decision Flow
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram showing the signal delivery path for a real SIGTERM arriving at the tracee. Shows: kernel intercepts → tracer wakes via waitpid → tracer calls PTRACE_SYSCALL with signal number → kernel delivers signal to tracee on resume. Contrasts with the SUPPRESS path (signal=0) showing the tracee never receives SIGTERM and hangs. Annotates which stop types require re-injection (real signals) vs which do not (SIGTRAP from ptrace machinery).

diagrams.title: Three-Level View: One syscall Stop, Hardware Through Application
diagrams.description: Three-column architecture diagram for a single read() syscall stop. Application column: tracer calls PTRACE_SYSCALL, blocks on waitpid, reads regs. Kernel column: saves task_struct registers, sets TASK_TRACED, wakes tracer wait queue, copy_to_user for GETREGS. Hardware column: CR3 switch, TLB pressure from address space switch, DRAM access for cold cache lines, ~1-10μs per context switch. Annotates the observer effect: 2 context switches per syscall.

modules.name: Argument Decoding
modules.description: Extends M1 with: x86_64 syscall name table (array indexed by syscall number), six-register argument extraction (rdi/rsi/rdx/r10/r8/r9), word-by-word string reader using PTRACE_PEEKDATA with errno disambiguation, NULL pointer handling, truncation with '...' suffix, bitmask flag decoding for open() and mmap(), syscall signature table mapping numbers to argument types, and strace-style formatted output. Does NOT handle multi-process, does NOT implement filtering or timing.

specs.inputs: struct user_regs_struct at entry stop; tracee PID for PTRACE_PEEKDATA calls; syscall number for table lookup
specs.outputs: strace-style formatted line fragment at entry stop: 'syscall_name(arg1, arg2, ...)'; completed with ' = retval\n' at exit stop
specs.abstractions: syscall_names[]: const char*[400] indexed by nr; syscall_sig_t {name, nargs, args[6]: arg_type_t[]}; arg_type_t enum (ARG_INT/UINT/FD/STR/HEX/OPEN_FLAGS/MMAP_PROT/MMAP_FLAGS/PTR); flag_entry_t {value: unsigned long, name: const char*}; read_string_from_tracee(pid, addr, out, max_len) -> int
error_categories: PTRACE_PEEKDATA returns -1 with errno!=0 (unmapped address, EFAULT)
NULL string pointer (valid for execve envp/argv)
String exceeds max_len (truncate + '...')
Unknown syscall number (bounds check on table)
NULL pointer passed as flag argument (print 0 or NULL)
errno inherited from previous call corrupting PEEKDATA error check
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded; all PTRACE_PEEKDATA calls are synchronous and blocking; errno must be cleared immediately before each call
performance_targets: PTRACE_PEEKDATA: 1-5μs per 8-byte word (kernel cross-address-space read + copy_to_user)
syscall_name() lookup: O(1) array index, single cache line read
Flag decoding: O(flags_in_table) linear scan, all flag tables < 1KB total

implementation_phases.name: Syscall name table and syscall_name() lookup function
Six-register argument extraction into syscall_info_t
PTRACE_PEEKDATA word-by-word string reader with errno disambiguation and alignment handling
Bitmask flag decoder + open() flags + mmap prot/flags tables
Syscall signature table + arg_type_t dispatch + strace-style entry/exit output

diagrams.title: PTRACE_PEEKDATA Word-by-Word String Reading: Alignment, Scan, and Null Detection
diagrams.description: Step-by-step algorithm diagram showing a string starting at unaligned address 0x7fff3a2b8003. Step 1: compute aligned_addr = addr & ~7ULL = 0x7fff3a2b8000, byte_offset = 3. Step 2: PTRACE_PEEKDATA reads 8 bytes, visualized as a byte array [0..7] with bytes 0-2 marked 'skip'. Step 3: scan bytes 3-7 for null. Step 4: advance aligned_addr by 8, reset offset to 0. Step 5: continue until null found or max_len reached. Shows the memory layout of the word on a little-endian x86_64 system with byte address annotations.

diagrams.title: PTRACE_PEEKDATA -1 Ambiguity: errno Disambiguation Protocol
diagrams.description: Decision flowchart showing the three-step protocol: (1) errno=0 before call, (2) word = PTRACE_PEEKDATA(...), (3) if word==-1 AND errno!=0 → real error; if word==-1 AND errno==0 → valid data 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF. Contrasts with the WRONG approach (check word==-1 without errno check) showing how 0xFF...FF bytes in a string get misclassified as read errors. Includes a concrete example byte sequence that triggers the false-positive.

diagrams.title: Syscall Argument Register Mapping: Signature Table to Registers
diagrams.description: Data flow diagram showing how a syscall signature like open(ARG_STR, ARG_OPEN_FLAGS, ARG_UINT) maps to registers: arg[0]=rdi→read_string_from_tracee, arg[1]=rsi→print_open_flags, arg[2]=rdx→printf %u. Explicitly marks the r10/rcx difference: arg[3] always uses r10, never rcx, with annotation explaining why (syscall instruction clobbers rcx for the return address). Shows the full six-register ordering for a six-argument syscall like mmap.

diagrams.title: Bitmask Flag Decoding: open() Flags Example with Access Mode Special Case
diagrams.description: Algorithm steps showing decode of flags=0x80241 (O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_CLOEXEC|O_TRUNC). Step 1: extract access mode via flags & O_ACCMODE (bits 0-1) → O_WRONLY. Step 2: clear access mode bits from remaining. Step 3: iterate flag_entry_t table, test (remaining & entry.value)==entry.value, print name, clear matched bits. Step 4: any residual bits printed as 0xHEX. Shows the O_RDONLY=0 special case where bit-AND always matches, requiring the pre-extraction of the access mode field first.

diagrams.title: Syscall Signature Table and arg_type_t Dispatch Architecture
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram showing syscall_sigs[] array of syscall_sig_t structs, each containing name, nargs, and args[6] of type arg_type_t. Shows the dispatch function print_arg() as a switch on arg_type_t fanning out to: printf %lld (ARG_INT), printf %u (ARG_UINT/FD), read_string_from_tracee+print_string_arg (ARG_STR), print_open_flags (ARG_OPEN_FLAGS), decode_flags(mmap_prot_flags) (ARG_MMAP_PROT), decode_flags(mmap_map_flags) (ARG_MMAP_FLAGS), printf 0x%llx (ARG_HEX/PTR). Includes the fallback path for unknown syscall numbers: print all six args as hex.

diagrams.title: Entry/Exit Output Assembly: Building One strace Line Across Two Stops
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram showing two stops for a single open() syscall. Entry stop: extract args → format '[pid N] open("/etc/passwd", O_RDONLY' into per-PID outbuf, no newline written. Exit stop: read rax retval → snprintf ') = 3\n' appended to outbuf → single fputs(outbuf, stderr) atomic write. Shows the WRONG interleaved output that occurs without the buffer (two separate fprintf calls). Annotates write() atomicity for PIPE_BUF (4096 bytes) guaranteeing no interleave for lines within that limit.

diagrams.title: Three-Level View: PTRACE_PEEKDATA for a Filename String
diagrams.description: Three-column diagram for reading '/etc/passwd' from tracee memory. Application: calls read_string_from_tracee(pid, 0x7fff3a2b8000, buf, 32) → 4 PTRACE_PEEKDATA calls. Kernel: for each call, switches to tracee's CR3, walks PML4→PDP→PD→PT to find physical frame, reads 8 bytes, copy_to_user to tracer buffer. Hardware: CR3 load = TLB context switch; page table walk = 4 memory accesses (potentially 4 DRAM misses at ~200 cycles each if cold); copy_to_user = 8-byte write to tracer page. Annotates total cost: 4 kernel transitions + 4 page walks = ~4-20μs for 11-byte string.

modules.name: Multi-Process and Fork Following
modules.description: Extends M2 with: PTRACE_O_TRACEFORK/VFORK/CLONE/EXEC/TRACESYSGOOD option setup, waitpid(-1) multi-process event loop, per-PID state hash map (open-addressing, Fibonacci hashing, 64-slot capacity), PTRACE_EVENT_* dispatcher using status>>16 extraction, PTRACE_GETEVENTMSG for new child PID retrieval, per-PID output buffering for atomic line writes, exec event memory-map reset, and PID-tagged output lines. Does NOT implement filtering, timing, or statistics.

specs.inputs: waitpid(-1) events from arbitrary traced PIDs; status words with PTRACE_EVENT high byte; PTRACE_GETEVENTMSG new child PID
specs.outputs: PID-tagged strace lines: '[pid N] syscall_name(args) = retval'; process lifecycle messages: '+++ exited with N +++', 'fork() = child_pid'
specs.abstractions: pid_map_t {slots[64]: pid_state_t}; pid_state_t {pid, in_syscall, current: syscall_info_t, active, outbuf[512], outbuf_len}; pid_hash(pid) using Fibonacci multiplicative hashing; pid_map_get()/pid_map_remove(); handle_ptrace_event() dispatcher; num_active counter
error_categories: PTRACE_GETEVENTMSG failure (tracee died between event and message read)
pid_map overflow (> 64 concurrent traced processes)
PTRACE_EVENT_EXEC with stale per-PID cached state (must reset)
Mid-syscall entry/exit toggle corruption from PTRACE_EVENT_FORK stop misclassification
Initial SIGSTOP for newly forked child confused with genuine SIGSTOP
PTRACE_SETOPTIONS failure on new child (options not inherited on all kernel versions)
waitpid ECHILD when all children have exited (loop termination)
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded tracer; waitpid(-1) serializes all events from all PIDs; per-PID state accessed sequentially; no locks needed; output atomicity via single fputs() per line
performance_targets: pid_map_get(): O(1) average, O(64) worst case linear probe; < 2 cache line touches for 64-slot table at 50% load
Full pid_map (64 slots × ~560 bytes): ~36KB, fits in L2 cache (256KB typical)
waitpid(-1) kernel overhead: O(N) child scan for N traced processes; negligible for N < 100
num_active counter: single int, always hot in register

implementation_phases.name: PTRACE_SETOPTIONS with TRACESYSGOOD+TRACEFORK+TRACEVFORK+TRACECLONE+TRACEEXEC
Per-PID hash map: pid_state_t, pid_map_t, pid_hash(), pid_map_get(), pid_map_remove()
waitpid(-1) loop with num_active counter and updated stop-type dispatch including event!=0 check
handle_ptrace_event(): FORK/VFORK/CLONE new child setup, EXEC state reset, EXIT logging
Per-PID output buffer for atomic line writes + PID-tagged output format

diagrams.title: pid_state_t Memory Layout: Byte Offsets and Cache Line Boundaries
diagrams.description: Byte-offset memory layout diagram for the full pid_state_t struct. Fields with offsets: [0] pid (4B), [4] in_syscall (4B), [8] current.syscall_nr (8B), [16] current.args[6] (48B), [64] current.retval (8B), [72] active (4B), [76] padding (4B), [80] outbuf[512] (512B), [592] outbuf_len (4B). Cache line boundaries at 64B intervals marked. Notes that the first cache line (pid+in_syscall+current) is the hot path accessed on every stop; outbuf is cold (accessed only at entry and exit of each syscall).

diagrams.title: pid_map_t Open-Addressing Hash Map: Fibonacci Hashing and Linear Probing
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram of pid_map_t showing 64 contiguous slots. Illustrates Fibonacci hash: h = pid * 2654435769u, index = h >> 26 (top 6 bits). Shows a collision scenario: PID 1234 hashes to slot 12, PID 5678 also hashes to slot 12 (probe to slot 13). Shows pid_map_get() inserting a new entry at the first inactive slot. Separately shows pid_map_remove() zeroing a slot and the tombstone problem: how a zeroed slot can break a probe chain for a third PID that collided at the same initial index.

diagrams.title: waitpid(-1) Multi-Process Event Dispatch Flowchart
diagrams.description: Complete flowchart of the multi-process event loop. Entry: waitpid(-1, &status, 0) → pid returned. Decode: WIFEXITED → log + pid_map_remove + num_active-- + continue; WIFSIGNALED → log + pid_map_remove + num_active-- + continue; WIFSTOPPED → decode sig and event. WIFSTOPPED branch: event=(status>>16)&0xff != 0 → handle_ptrace_event (NO toggle flip); sig==(SIGTRAP|0x80) → flip toggle + handle_syscall_stop; sig==SIGTRAP → resume with signal 0; otherwise → resume with signal=sig (re-inject). Loop termination: num_active==0 OR ECHILD errno.

diagrams.title: PTRACE_EVENT_FORK Sequence: Parent Stop, Child Birth, Dual Resume
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram across tracer, kernel, parent-tracee, and child-tracee. Shows: (1) parent syscall-entry stop for fork() — toggle flips to in_syscall=1; (2) PTRACE_EVENT_FORK stop for parent — event check fires, NO toggle flip; (3) PTRACE_GETEVENTMSG → new_pid; (4) pid_map_get(new_pid) initializes child state with in_syscall=0; (5) PTRACE_SETOPTIONS on new child; (6) PTRACE_SYSCALL resumes new child; (7) PTRACE_SYSCALL resumes parent; (8) parent syscall-exit stop for fork() returning child_pid — toggle flips to in_syscall=0. Shows child's initial SIGSTOP stop as a separate event that must be consumed.

diagrams.title: PTRACE_EVENT_EXEC: Memory Map Replacement and Per-PID State Reset
diagrams.description: Before/After diagram showing pid_state_t for PID 1234 before exec (has cached syscall_nr=59, args pointing into old address space, in_syscall=1) and after PTRACE_EVENT_EXEC handling (current zeroed, in_syscall=0). Annotates why all cached string pointers are invalid (old page table is gone, new program loaded). Shows the timing: EXEC event fires AFTER kernel replaces address space but BEFORE first instruction of new program. Notes the subsequent syscall-exit stop for execve itself and how the tracer handles it.

diagrams.title: Per-PID Output Buffer: Preventing Line Interleaving in Multi-Process Trace
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison of two scenarios. WRONG scenario: PID 1234 entry stop → fprintf('[pid 1234] open('); PID 1235 complete syscall → fprintf('[pid 1235] getpid() = 1235\n'); PID 1234 exit stop → fprintf(') = 3\n'). Result: mangled output. CORRECT scenario: PID 1234 entry stop → snprintf into state->outbuf (no write to stderr); PID 1235 complete → fputs(1235_outbuf) single atomic write; PID 1234 exit stop → complete 1234_outbuf → fputs(1234_outbuf) single atomic write. Shows that write() atomicity for PIPE_BUF guarantees no interleave.

diagrams.title: Entry/Exit Toggle Correctness Across Interleaved PIDs
diagrams.description: Timeline diagram showing events from PID 1234 and PID 1235 interleaved. Events in order: PID1234 entry(write), PID1235 entry(read), PID1234 exit(write=13), PID1235 exit(read=512). Each event shows the per-PID in_syscall state at that moment: PID1234.in_syscall: 0→1→1→0; PID1235.in_syscall: 0→1→0 (independent). Demonstrates that because state is per-PID, neither toggle is corrupted by the other PID's events. Contrasts with a SINGLE global in_syscall toggle which would be: 0→1→0→1, misclassifying PID1234's exit as an entry.

modules.name: Filtering and Statistics
modules.description: Extends M3 with: syscall name filter (-e trace=name,...) applied at exit stop after timing, per-syscall CLOCK_MONOTONIC timing with observer-effect documentation, global syscall_stats_t[400] statistics accumulator, qsort-based summary table generation (-c flag), output redirection to file (-o flag) via global FILE*, PTRACE_ATTACH lifecycle for running process attachment (-p PID) including mid-syscall state handling, and clean PTRACE_DETACH on SIGINT via volatile sig_atomic_t flag and detach_all() loop.

specs.inputs: Command-line options: -e trace=..., -o filename, -p PID, -c; clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC) timestamps; SIGINT signal for detach
specs.outputs: Filtered trace output to FILE* (stderr or file); on exit: optional summary table sorted by cumulative time with columns: syscall, calls, errors, usecs/total, %time
specs.abstractions: syscall_filter_t {names: const char**, count: int}; syscall_stats_t {call_count: uint64_t, error_count: uint64_t, total_ns: uint64_t}; g_stats[400]: syscall_stats_t; tracer_opts_t {attach_pid, output_file, filter_str, show_stats}; timespec_diff_ns(start, end) -> uint64_t; filter_matches(syscall_nr) -> int; stats_record(nr, retval, ns); print_statistics(FILE*); detach_all(pid_map_t*); volatile sig_atomic_t g_interrupted
error_categories: PTRACE_ATTACH requires waitpid before any subsequent ptrace call (ESRCH if skipped)
Mid-syscall attach: first stop may be exit stop without seen entry stop (partial line handling)
CLOCK_REALTIME backward NTP step producing negative duration (prevented by CLOCK_MONOTONIC)
PTRACE_DETACH on running process returns ESRCH (must send SIGSTOP+waitpid first)
filter_str parsing: strtok modifies string in place (must strdup before tokenizing)
Output file fopen failure
stats table overflow for syscall_nr >= 400 (bounds check required)
Negative elapsed_ns from incorrect clock (defensive clamp to 0)
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded tracer; SIGINT handler sets g_interrupted flag only (async-signal-safe); all cleanup in main loop; g_stats updates are sequential (no races); FILE* writes are sequential (no concurrent writers)
performance_targets: clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC): ~5-20ns via vDSO (no syscall, reads kernel-mapped page)
stats_record(): 3 uint64_t increments + bounds check = ~5 cycles, always in L1 cache
g_stats[400]: 9,600 bytes, fits entirely in L1 cache (32KB typical)
print_statistics() sort: O(N log N) qsort over at most ~335 entries = negligible
filter_matches(): O(filter_count) string comparisons; typical filter_count <= 10

implementation_phases.name: CLOCK_MONOTONIC timing: entry_time in pid_state_t, timespec_diff_ns(), stats_record()
Syscall filter: parse_filter(), filter_matches(), integration at exit stop (after timing, before print)
Statistics accumulator and print_statistics() with qsort and formatted table output
Output redirection: g_output FILE*, -o flag, fopen/fclose lifecycle
PTRACE_ATTACH for -p PID: attach_to_process(), mid-syscall state handling, options setup
SIGINT handler, g_interrupted flag, detach_all() with SIGSTOP+waitpid guard, argument parsing with getopt

diagrams.title: Syscall Filter Architecture: Trace All, Print Selectively
diagrams.description: Data flow diagram showing that ALL syscalls still reach the PTRACE_SYSCALL+waitpid loop (entry/exit toggle is maintained for 100% of syscalls). At the exit stop, after clock_gettime and stats_record, filter_matches(syscall_nr) is evaluated. FALSE path: discard outbuf, return (no print). TRUE path: complete outbuf and fputs. Annotates WHY filtering cannot skip interception: the entry/exit toggle would desynchronize causing next stop to be misclassified.

diagrams.title: CLOCK_MONOTONIC vs CLOCK_REALTIME: Why the Wrong Clock Breaks Profiling
diagrams.description: Timeline diagram showing an NTP adjustment event occurring during a tracing session. CLOCK_REALTIME line: jumps backward by 50ms mid-trace; a syscall whose entry_time is after the jump and exit_time before it measures as -50ms duration. CLOCK_MONOTONIC line: strictly increasing, same NTP event has zero effect. Annotates the vDSO fast path: both clocks are read via kernel-mapped page without ring-0 transition (~5-20ns). Shows concrete example: read() blocking for 300ms on disk I/O measures correctly as ~300ms under CLOCK_MONOTONIC regardless of NTP.

diagrams.title: Syscall Timing: What You Actually Measure vs True Syscall Cost
diagrams.description: Annotated timeline for a single getpid() call under ptrace. T0: tracee executes syscall instruction → entry stop fires → tracer wakes. T1: tracer calls clock_gettime(entry_time). T2: tracer calls PTRACE_SYSCALL → context switch to tracee. T3: kernel executes getpid() (~100ns). T4: exit stop fires → tracer wakes. T5: tracer calls clock_gettime(exit_time). Measured = T5-T1 = (T2-T1) + (T4-T2) + (T5-T4) = tracer overhead + actual syscall + tracer overhead. For getpid: actual=100ns, measured=5-50μs. Labels this the observer effect and documents what IS and IS NOT meaningful in the statistics output.

diagrams.title: syscall_stats_t Array: Memory Layout and Cache Behavior
diagrams.description: Memory layout diagram of g_stats[400]. Each syscall_stats_t entry is 24 bytes (call_count: 8B, error_count: 8B, total_ns: 8B). Total: 400×24 = 9,600 bytes. Shows that common syscalls (read=0, write=1, open=2, close=3, mmap=9, openat=257) are accessed on nearly every traced program, keeping their entries in L1 cache. Rare syscalls (entries 200-399) are cold. Annotates that stats_record() is 3 integer increments + 1 bounds check = ~5 CPU cycles, dominated by cache hit (L1: 4 cycles) not computation.

diagrams.title: Statistics Table Generation: Collection, Sort, and Display Pipeline
diagrams.description: Algorithm steps showing the print_statistics() pipeline. Step 1: scan g_stats[0..399], collect non-zero entries into sort_entry_t[] array, accumulate total_time_ns. Step 2: qsort(entries, count, compare_by_time_desc). Step 3: print header row. Step 4: for each sorted entry, format row with call_count, error_count, total_ns/1000 (usecs), percentage = 100.0*entry.total_ns/total_time_ns. Step 5: print footer totals. Shows the output format matching strace -c. Notes that percentage is computed at display time from accumulated totals, not maintained as a running float (avoids floating-point accumulation error).

diagrams.title: PTRACE_ATTACH Lifecycle: Attach, Stop, Options, Mid-Syscall State
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram for './my_strace -p 1234' attaching to a running Python process. Step 1: ptrace(PTRACE_ATTACH, 1234) → kernel sends SIGSTOP to Python asynchronously, returns immediately. Step 2: waitpid(1234) blocks → Python receives SIGSTOP, stops → waitpid returns WIFSTOPPED/SIGSTOP. Step 3: ptrace(PTRACE_SETOPTIONS, 1234, opts). Step 4: pid_map_get(1234) with in_syscall=0. Step 5: ptrace(PTRACE_SYSCALL, 1234, 0, 0). Step 6: first event — may be EXIT stop if Python was mid-syscall (e.g., blocked in read()). Shows the mid-syscall fallback path: print '<attached mid-syscall: syscall_name> = retval' without flipping toggle, allowing next pair to synchronize correctly.

diagrams.title: Clean Detach on SIGINT: Signal Handler, Flag, and detach_all() Dance
diagrams.description: State machine and sequence diagram combined. Signal handler state: SIGINT arrives → sigint_handler() sets g_interrupted=1 (async-signal-safe, no other work). Main loop state: each loop iteration checks g_interrupted before blocking; if set → call detach_all(). detach_all() sequence for each active PID: (1) try ptrace(PTRACE_DETACH, pid, 0, 0); (2) if ESRCH (process running) → kill(pid, SIGSTOP) → waitpid(pid) → ptrace(PTRACE_DETACH, pid, 0, 0); (3) log 'Detached from pid N'. Shows SA_RESTART=0 so waitpid returns EINTR on SIGINT, allowing the loop to check g_interrupted without deadlocking.

diagrams.title: Argument Parsing: getopt Option Flow and tracer_opts_t Population
diagrams.description: Data flow diagram showing getopt() consuming argv: -o filename → opts.output_file; -p PID → opts.attach_pid (atoi + validate >0); -e trace=X,Y → strip 'trace=' prefix → opts.filter_str; -c → opts.show_stats=1; '+' prefix in optstring stops processing at first non-option (argv[optind] = program to exec). Shows post-parse validation: if attach_pid==0 AND optind>=argc → error (no program specified). Shows strdup(filter_str) before parse_filter() to prevent strtok modification of argv memory.

diagrams.title: Complete M4 Module Architecture: Components and Data Dependencies
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram showing all M4 components and their relationships. Central: the event loop (handle_syscall_stop). Feeds into: clock_gettime → timespec_diff_ns → stats_record(g_stats). Then: filter_matches(g_filter) → conditional: complete outbuf → fputs(g_output). Global state nodes: g_stats[400] (feeds print_statistics), g_filter (feeds filter_matches), g_output FILE* (feeds all fputs), g_interrupted volatile (feeds loop exit condition), g_pid_map (from M3, feeds pid_map_get). Startup flow: parse_args → tracer_opts_t → branch to attach_to_process OR fork+run_child. Shutdown flow: g_interrupted=1 → detach_all → print_statistics → fclose.

tdd_accumulated_md:
# TDD

A ptrace-based syscall interception engine for x86_64 Linux that progressively reveals kernel-mediated process observation: from a single-process entry/exit toggle through full argument decoding via cross-address-space memory reads, to a multi-process event dispatcher with per-PID state machines, culminating in production-quality filtering, monotonic timing, and attach/detach lifecycle management. Every design decision is grounded in the hardware cost of context switching, TLB pressure, and the observer effect on measurement.



<!-- TDD_MOD_ID: build-strace-m1 -->
# Technical Design Specification: Basic ptrace Syscall Intercept
**Module ID**: `build-strace-m1`  
**Language**: C (binding)  
**Target**: x86_64 Linux, kernel ≥ 4.x  
**Scope**: Single-process ptrace tracing lifecycle — entry/exit toggle, register reads, error detection, signal re-injection. No argument decoding, no fork-following, no filtering, no statistics.
---
## 1. Module Charter
This module implements the complete tracing lifecycle for a single child process. It forks a child, has the child call `PTRACE_TRACEME` and exec the target program, then runs a parent-side `waitpid` loop that intercepts every system call at both entry and exit. At each stop the module classifies the stop type (syscall entry, syscall exit, signal delivery, or process termination), reads the appropriate registers (`orig_rax` at entry, `rax` at exit), and emits one formatted line per completed syscall to `stderr`.
This module does **not** read or decode argument registers (`rdi`, `rsi`, `rdx`, `r10`, `r8`, `r9`). It does **not** follow child processes created via `fork`, `vfork`, or `clone`. It does **not** implement syscall name lookup (syscall numbers print as integers). It does **not** implement filtering, timing, or statistics. It does **not** set `PTRACE_O_TRACEFORK`, `PTRACE_O_TRACEVFORK`, or `PTRACE_O_TRACECLONE` options — those belong to Milestone 3.
**Upstream dependency**: None — this is the root module. It receives `argv[]` from `main`.  
**Downstream consumers**: Milestone 2 extends `handle_syscall_stop` to decode argument registers; Milestone 3 replaces the single `tracee_state_t` with a per-PID hash map.
**Invariants that must always hold**:
1. The `in_syscall` toggle alternates strictly between 0 and 1 on every syscall stop — never two consecutive 0s or two consecutive 1s.
2. `orig_rax` is read only at entry stops; `rax` is read only at exit stops.
3. Every signal-delivery stop results in signal re-injection via the last argument to `PTRACE_SYSCALL`; signal zero is never passed for a non-SIGTRAP stop.
4. After `WIFEXITED` or `WIFSIGNALED`, the tracing loop exits cleanly with no further ptrace calls on that PID.
---
## 2. File Structure
Create files in this order:
```
my_strace/
├── 1  Makefile
├── 2  my_strace.c          # all implementation for this milestone
└── 3  test_basic.sh        # shell-based acceptance tests
```
Everything lives in `my_strace.c` for this milestone. Milestone 2 will extract `syscall_table.h`; that split is deferred intentionally. Keeping a single file now maximises legibility during the first implementation pass.
---
## 3. Complete Data Model
### 3.1 `tracee_state_t` — Per-Tracee State Machine
```c
/*
 * tracee_state_t: complete per-tracee state for the entry/exit toggle.
 *
 * Memory layout on x86_64 (both fields are naturally aligned):
 *   Offset 0x00  in_syscall         int   (4 bytes)
 *   Offset 0x04  current_syscall_nr long  (8 bytes)
 *   ─────────────────────────────────────────────
 *   Total: 12 bytes. Struct fits entirely within a single 64-byte
 *   cache line. Stack-allocated; no heap allocation.
 *
 * Compiler may add 4 bytes of padding after in_syscall to align
 * current_syscall_nr to 8 bytes. sizeof(tracee_state_t) == 16 on
 * x86_64 with standard ABI alignment rules.
 */
typedef struct {
    int  in_syscall;          /* 0 = last stop was exit (or not yet started)
                               * 1 = last stop was entry
                               * Toggle on EVERY syscall stop. */
    long current_syscall_nr;  /* Syscall number saved at entry stop.
                               * Read from orig_rax. Valid only when
                               * in_syscall transitions from 1→0 at exit.
                               * -1 = uninitialized / never set. */
} tracee_state_t;
```
**Why `int in_syscall` not `bool`**: C99's `_Bool` from `<stdbool.h>` would work, but `int` avoids any implicit promotion surprises when printing diagnostics and matches the ptrace convention of using integers for all state flags.
**Why `long current_syscall_nr`**: `orig_rax` in `struct user_regs_struct` is declared as `unsigned long long`. Storing into `long` is safe because x86_64 has at most ~335 syscalls (all fit in a signed `long`) and negative values are used only as the sentinel `-1` for "uninitialized."
{{DIAGRAM:tdd-diag-1}}
### 3.2 Register Fields Used From `struct user_regs_struct`
Defined in `<sys/user.h>`. The full struct has 27 fields (216 bytes on x86_64). This module reads only:
```
Field name        Type                Byte offset    Used at
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
orig_rax          unsigned long long  0x78 (120)     Entry stop only
rax               unsigned long long  0x50 (80)      Exit stop only
```
All other fields are present in the struct and populated by `PTRACE_GETREGS` but are ignored by this module. Milestone 2 reads `rdi` (0x70), `rsi` (0x68), `rdx` (0x60), `r10` (0x28), `r8` (0x48), `r9` (0x40).
{{DIAGRAM:tdd-diag-2}}
### 3.3 `waitpid` Status Word Semantics
The `status` integer returned via `waitpid`'s second argument encodes multiple distinct event types in a packed bit layout. This module uses only the POSIX macros — never raw bit manipulation of `status`:
```
Macro               Meaning                             Action
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WIFEXITED(s)        Process called exit() / returned    Break tracing loop
WEXITSTATUS(s)      Exit code (valid if WIFEXITED)      Print "+++ exited ..."
WIFSIGNALED(s)      Process killed by unhandled signal  Break tracing loop
WTERMSIG(s)         Signal number (valid if WIFSIGNALED) Print "+++ killed ..."
WIFSTOPPED(s)       Process stopped (ptrace or job ctrl) Inspect WSTOPSIG
WSTOPSIG(s)         Stop signal (valid if WIFSTOPPED)   Classify stop type
```
Stop classification from `WSTOPSIG(s)` in this milestone (pre-`PTRACE_O_TRACESYSGOOD`):
```
WSTOPSIG value     Interpretation            Required action
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SIGTRAP (5)        Syscall entry or exit     Toggle + read registers
anything else      Signal-delivery stop      Re-inject via PTRACE_SYSCALL
```
**Note**: This classification is intentionally simplified for Milestone 1. Milestone 3 enables `PTRACE_O_TRACESYSGOOD` which changes syscall stops to `SIGTRAP | 0x80` (value 133), making them unambiguous. For now, `SIGTRAP` is the only sentinel.
---
## 4. Interface Contracts
### 4.1 `run_child(char *argv[])` — Child Side Setup
```c
static void run_child(char *argv[]);
```
**Parameters**: `argv` — null-terminated array of strings. `argv[0]` is the program to exec; subsequent elements are its arguments. Must contain at least one element. If `argv[0]` is NULL, behavior is undefined.
**Returns**: Never returns on success. Returns only on `execvp` failure.
**Preconditions**: Must be called in the child process after `fork()`. Must be called before any exec on this child.
**Postconditions on success**: None — the process image is replaced by `execvp`.
**On failure**:
- `ptrace(PTRACE_TRACEME)` fails → `perror("ptrace(PTRACE_TRACEME)")` then `exit(1)`. Parent's `waitpid` will see `WIFEXITED` with code 1.
- `execvp` fails → `perror("execvp")` then `exit(1)`. The errno printed is the errno from `execvp` (e.g., `ENOENT` if program not found, `EACCES` if not executable).
**Side effects**: 
- `PTRACE_TRACEME`: registers this process as a willing tracee with its parent as tracer.
- After `execvp` succeeds: kernel delivers `SIGTRAP` to the child before it executes a single instruction of the new program. This is the first stop the parent will observe.
**Critical ordering constraint**: `PTRACE_TRACEME` MUST be called before `execvp`. After `execvp` replaces the process image, the setup code is gone. The correct sequence is: `fork()` → child calls `PTRACE_TRACEME` → child calls `execvp`.
### 4.2 `run_tracer(pid_t child_pid)` — Parent Side Event Loop
```c
static void run_tracer(pid_t child_pid);
```
**Parameters**: `child_pid` — PID of the child created by `fork()`, expected to be in the post-`PTRACE_TRACEME` state, about to exec.
**Returns**: `void`. Returns when the child process terminates (either normally or by signal).
**Preconditions**: `child_pid` must be a child of the calling process. The child must have called `PTRACE_TRACEME` before exec.
**Algorithm overview**: 
1. Call `waitpid(child_pid, &status, 0)` once to consume the initial post-exec `SIGTRAP`. Do not treat this as a syscall stop — it is the kernel signaling that exec completed. Do not flip the toggle.
2. Enter the main loop: call `PTRACE_SYSCALL` then `waitpid` in alternation.
3. On each `waitpid` return, dispatch: `WIFEXITED` → print exit message and return; `WIFSIGNALED` → print kill message and return; `WIFSTOPPED` → classify and handle.
4. For `SIGTRAP` stops: flip `state.in_syscall`, call `handle_syscall_stop`.
5. For all other stops: re-inject the signal number via `ptrace(PTRACE_SYSCALL, child_pid, NULL, stop_sig)` and `continue` (skipping the `PTRACE_SYSCALL` at the loop top).
**Error handling within loop**: If `ptrace(PTRACE_SYSCALL, ...)` or `waitpid` returns -1 for reasons other than `EINTR`, print the error with `perror` and `break` from the loop.
**`EINTR` from `waitpid`**: If `waitpid` returns -1 with `errno == EINTR` (signal delivered to the tracer itself), call `waitpid` again — do not break. Do not issue another `PTRACE_SYSCALL` before the `waitpid` completes.
### 4.3 `handle_syscall_stop(pid_t pid, tracee_state_t *state)` — Register Read and Output
```c
static void handle_syscall_stop(pid_t pid, tracee_state_t *state);
```
**Parameters**:
- `pid` — PID of the stopped tracee. Must be currently stopped at a ptrace stop.
- `state` — pointer to the per-tracee toggle state. `state->in_syscall` has already been flipped by the caller before this function is invoked.
**Returns**: `void`. On `PTRACE_GETREGS` failure, prints error with `perror` and returns without printing a syscall line.
**At entry stop** (`state->in_syscall == 1` after caller's flip):
- Call `ptrace(PTRACE_GETREGS, pid, NULL, &regs)`.
- Save `(long)regs.orig_rax` into `state->current_syscall_nr`.
- **Do not print anything** — output is deferred to the exit stop so entry and return value appear on one line.
**At exit stop** (`state->in_syscall == 0` after caller's flip):
- Call `ptrace(PTRACE_GETREGS, pid, NULL, &regs)`.
- Read `retval = (long)regs.rax`.
- Use `state->current_syscall_nr` (set at entry) as the syscall number.
- Call `print_syscall_result(state->current_syscall_nr, retval)`.
**Why no output at entry**: Real strace writes `syscall_name(args) = retval` as one line. Splitting across two stops requires buffering. For this milestone, we defer entirely: no output until exit.
### 4.4 `print_syscall_result(long syscall_nr, long retval)` — Output Formatting
```c
static void print_syscall_result(long syscall_nr, long retval);
```
**Parameters**:
- `syscall_nr` — syscall number from `orig_rax`. Any `long` value; negative values (invalid syscall numbers) print as-is.
- `retval` — return value from `rax`. Interpreted as signed.
**Output format** (to `stderr`, always newline-terminated):
```
# Success case:
syscall(N) = RETVAL\n
# Error case (retval in [-4095, -1]):
syscall(N) = -1 ERRNO_NAME (strerror description)\n
```
**Examples**:
```
syscall(0) = 512
syscall(2) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or directory)
syscall(60) = 0
syscall(999) = -1 ENOSYS (Function not implemented)
```
**Error branch condition**: `is_error_return(retval)` returns true. Then: `err = (int)(-retval)`, print `errno_name(err)` and `strerror(err)`. Note: `strerror(err)` is locale-sensitive but always valid for valid errno values.
### 4.5 `is_error_return(long retval)` — Error Range Predicate
```c
static int is_error_return(long retval);
```
**Returns**: 1 if `retval` is in the range `[-4095, -1]` inclusive; 0 otherwise.
**Implementation**:
```c
static int is_error_return(long retval) {
    return (retval >= -4095L && retval < 0);
}
```
**Why -4095 not -4096**: The Linux kernel uses the range `[-MAX_ERRNO, -1]` where `MAX_ERRNO` is defined as 4095 in `include/linux/errno.h`. Using -4096 is also common in documentation (strace itself uses -4096), and is safe in practice because no errno value is 4096. Either bound is acceptable; -4095 is technically precise.
**Critical**: Do NOT use `retval < 0` alone. The syscall `mmap` returns very large addresses as unsigned values that, when cast to signed `long`, are large negative numbers far outside [-4095, -1]. The bounded range distinguishes error codes from valid large pointer returns.
### 4.6 `errno_name(int err)` — Errno Symbol Table
```c
static const char *errno_name(int err);
```
**Parameters**: `err` — a positive errno value (already negated from the raw return value).
**Returns**: A string constant like `"ENOENT"`. For unknown errno values, returns a string like `"E42"` from a static buffer (not thread-safe, but the tracer is single-threaded).
**Required entries** (minimum table — expand as observed in practice):
```c
/* Required errno mappings — add more as encountered */
static const char *errno_name(int err) {
    switch (err) {
        case EPERM:    return "EPERM";
        case ENOENT:   return "ENOENT";
        case ESRCH:    return "ESRCH";
        case EINTR:    return "EINTR";
        case EIO:      return "EIO";
        case ENXIO:    return "ENXIO";
        case EBADF:    return "EBADF";
        case ECHILD:   return "ECHILD";
        case EAGAIN:   return "EAGAIN";
        case ENOMEM:   return "ENOMEM";
        case EACCES:   return "EACCES";
        case EFAULT:   return "EFAULT";
        case EBUSY:    return "EBUSY";
        case EEXIST:   return "EEXIST";
        case ENODEV:   return "ENODEV";
        case ENOTDIR:  return "ENOTDIR";
        case EISDIR:   return "EISDIR";
        case EINVAL:   return "EINVAL";
        case ENFILE:   return "ENFILE";
        case EMFILE:   return "EMFILE";
        case ENOTTY:   return "ENOTTY";
        case EFBIG:    return "EFBIG";
        case ENOSPC:   return "ENOSPC";
        case ESPIPE:   return "ESPIPE";
        case EROFS:    return "EROFS";
        case EPIPE:    return "EPIPE";
        case ERANGE:   return "ERANGE";
        case ENOSYS:   return "ENOSYS";
        case ENOTSUP:  return "ENOTSUP";
        case ELOOP:    return "ELOOP";
        case ENAMETOOLONG: return "ENAMETOOLONG";
        case ENOTEMPTY: return "ENOTEMPTY";
        default: {
            static char buf[16];
            snprintf(buf, sizeof(buf), "E%d", err);
            return buf;
        }
    }
}
```
**Static buffer safety**: The `default` branch uses a `static char buf[16]`. This is safe because the tracer is single-threaded and `errno_name` is called at most once per `print_syscall_result` call, which completes before any subsequent call.
---
## 5. Algorithm Specification
### 5.1 Main Tracing Loop — Full State Machine
{{DIAGRAM:tdd-diag-3}}
```
Input:  child_pid (valid child PID; child has called PTRACE_TRACEME and exec'd)
Output: formatted lines to stderr; returns when child terminates
State:  tracee_state_t state = { .in_syscall = 0, .current_syscall_nr = -1 }
STEP 1 — Consume initial exec stop:
  Call waitpid(child_pid, &status, 0).
  If WIFEXITED(status) or WIFSIGNALED(status):
    Print "Child terminated before tracing loop." to stderr.
    Return.
  // If WIFSTOPPED: normal. The stop is from the kernel's post-exec SIGTRAP.
  // DO NOT flip state.in_syscall. DO NOT call handle_syscall_stop.
  // DO NOT call PTRACE_GETREGS. This stop is setup, not a syscall stop.
STEP 2 — Main loop (repeat until break):
  2a. Issue resume:
      rc = ptrace(PTRACE_SYSCALL, child_pid, NULL, 0)
      If rc < 0:
        perror("ptrace(PTRACE_SYSCALL)")
        break
  2b. Wait for next event:
      pid = waitpid(child_pid, &status, 0)
      If pid < 0:
        If errno == EINTR: goto step 2b (retry waitpid without re-issuing PTRACE_SYSCALL)
        perror("waitpid")
        break
  2c. Dispatch on status:
      If WIFEXITED(status):
        fprintf(stderr, "+++ exited with %d +++\n", WEXITSTATUS(status))
        break
      If WIFSIGNALED(status):
        fprintf(stderr, "+++ killed by signal %d (%s) +++\n",
                WTERMSIG(status), strsignal(WTERMSIG(status)))
        break
      If WIFSTOPPED(status):
        stop_sig = WSTOPSIG(status)
        If stop_sig == SIGTRAP:
          // Syscall stop — toggle and handle
          state.in_syscall = !state.in_syscall
          handle_syscall_stop(child_pid, &state)
          // Fall through to top of loop (next PTRACE_SYSCALL at step 2a)
        Else:
          // Signal-delivery stop — re-inject
          rc = ptrace(PTRACE_SYSCALL, child_pid, NULL, (void*)(uintptr_t)stop_sig)
          If rc < 0:
            perror("ptrace(PTRACE_SYSCALL) [reinject]")
            break
          // CRITICAL: 'continue' to skip step 2a — PTRACE_SYSCALL already issued
          continue to STEP 2b
Output invariant after loop: state may be in any toggle position; no cleanup required.
```
{{DIAGRAM:tdd-diag-4}}
### 5.2 Entry/Exit Toggle State Machine
```
States:    OUTSIDE_SYSCALL (in_syscall=0), INSIDE_SYSCALL (in_syscall=1)
Initial:   OUTSIDE_SYSCALL
Transition table:
  Current state       Event                    Action                   Next state
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  OUTSIDE_SYSCALL     SIGTRAP stop             in_syscall=1             INSIDE_SYSCALL
                                               save orig_rax → nr
                                               (no print)
  INSIDE_SYSCALL      SIGTRAP stop             in_syscall=0             OUTSIDE_SYSCALL
                                               read rax → retval
                                               print line
  Either              signal-delivery stop     re-inject stop_sig       unchanged
  Either              WIFEXITED/WIFSIGNALED    break loop               (terminal)
Illegal transitions (must never occur):
  OUTSIDE_SYSCALL  →  OUTSIDE_SYSCALL  (two consecutive exit stops)
  INSIDE_SYSCALL   →  INSIDE_SYSCALL   (two consecutive entry stops)
```
The toggle is the ONLY mechanism distinguishing entry from exit stops. The kernel provides no flag in the `waitpid` status word that identifies entry vs. exit. If the toggle becomes desynchronized (e.g., an extra SIGTRAP slip through), all subsequent reads will be from the wrong stop type, producing invalid output. Resynchronization is not attempted in this module; Milestone 3 adds `PTRACE_O_TRACESYSGOOD` and could in principle use `PTRACE_GET_SYSCALL_INFO` (kernel 5.3+) to verify.
### 5.3 Signal Re-injection Algorithm
```
Input: stop_sig = WSTOPSIG(status), where stop_sig != SIGTRAP
STEP 1: Validate re-injection necessity.
  stop_sig is a real signal intended for the tracee.
  It was intercepted by ptrace before delivery.
  The tracee has NOT yet received it.
STEP 2: Call ptrace(PTRACE_SYSCALL, child_pid, NULL, stop_sig).
  The last argument is the signal to deliver upon resumption.
  Passing stop_sig causes the kernel to deliver the signal to the
  tracee's signal queue when the tracee resumes.
  Passing 0 would SUPPRESS the signal — DO NOT DO THIS for real signals.
STEP 3: 'continue' in the loop body.
  We have already issued PTRACE_SYSCALL (with signal).
  The loop top issues another PTRACE_SYSCALL (without signal).
  Issuing two PTRACE_SYSCALLs before waitpid is harmless: the second
  call resumes an already-running process and returns ESRCH.
  BUT: it is cleaner and correct to 'continue' to step 2b and skip step 2a.
```

## 6. Error Handling Matrix
| Error Condition | Detected By | Recovery | User-Visible Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| `fork()` returns -1 | return value check in `main` | `perror("fork")`, `return 1` | Yes: printed to stderr |
| `ptrace(PTRACE_TRACEME)` fails | return value < 0 in child | `perror(...)`, `exit(1)` | Yes: child stderr, parent sees exit code 1 |
| `execvp` fails (program not found) | return from `execvp` | `perror("execvp")`, `exit(1)` | Yes: child stderr; parent sees WIFEXITED code 1 |
| Initial `waitpid` fails | return < 0 | `perror("waitpid (initial)")`, return | Yes: to stderr |
| Child exits before loop starts | WIFEXITED/WIFSIGNALED after initial waitpid | Print message, return | Yes: "Child terminated before tracing loop." |
| `ptrace(PTRACE_SYSCALL)` fails in loop | return < 0 | `perror(...)`, break loop | Yes: to stderr |
| `waitpid` in loop returns -1 with `EINTR` | `errno == EINTR` | Retry `waitpid` (no new PTRACE_SYSCALL) | No |
| `waitpid` in loop returns -1, non-EINTR | `errno != EINTR` | `perror("waitpid")`, break loop | Yes: to stderr |
| `PTRACE_GETREGS` fails | return < 0 | `perror(...)`, return from handler | Yes: to stderr; that syscall line is skipped |
| `PTRACE_SYSCALL` fails during reinject | return < 0 | `perror(...)`, break loop | Yes: to stderr |
| Tracee exits normally | `WIFEXITED(status)` | Print exit line, break | Yes: "+++ exited with N +++" |
| Tracee killed by signal | `WIFSIGNALED(status)` | Print kill line, break | Yes: "+++ killed by signal N +++" |
| Unknown `waitpid` status (not stopped/exited/signaled) | None of the POSIX macros return true | Silently skip — fall to loop top | No |
| `syscall_nr` out of valid range | Checked in `errno_name` and future name table | Print numeric "syscall(N)" | No (partial) |
**No error path leaves the child process in a permanently frozen state**: on any `break`, the tracer exits, the kernel auto-detaches the tracee, and the tracee resumes or terminates. No cleanup of ptrace state is required on the tracer side.

## 7. Implementation Sequence with Checkpoints
### Phase 1: Fork/exec/PTRACE_TRACEME Skeleton (1–2 hours)
Implement `main()`, `run_child()`, and a stub `run_tracer()` that only consumes the initial stop and prints "attached."
```c
/* Files to create: my_strace.c with these includes */
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/ptrace.h>
#include <sys/types.h>
#include <sys/wait.h>
#include <sys/user.h>
#include <errno.h>
#include <signal.h>
```
Implement in order:
1. `main()` — parse `argc/argv`, call `fork()`, branch on child/parent.
2. `run_child()` — `PTRACE_TRACEME`, `execvp`, error paths.
3. `run_tracer()` stub — single `waitpid`, print "Attached. First stop received.", return.
**Checkpoint 1**: `gcc -Wall -Wextra -o my_strace my_strace.c && ./my_strace /bin/true`
Expected: prints "Attached. First stop received." then returns with exit code 0. No crash, no hang.
If it hangs: the child did not call `PTRACE_TRACEME` before exec, or `waitpid` is called with wrong PID.  
If it crashes with SIGSEGV: `argv` indexing is wrong (`argv+1` vs `argv`).
### Phase 2: waitpid Loop with Exit/Signal/Stop Dispatch (1–2 hours)
Replace the stub `run_tracer` with the full loop: `PTRACE_SYSCALL` → `waitpid` → dispatch on `WIFEXITED`/`WIFSIGNALED`/`WIFSTOPPED`. No toggle yet — just call `PTRACE_SYSCALL` on every `SIGTRAP` stop without doing anything else.
**Checkpoint 2**: `./my_strace /bin/true`
Expected: loop runs silently through all syscalls, terminates with `+++ exited with 0 +++`. No hang.
`./my_strace /bin/false`  
Expected: `+++ exited with 1 +++`.
`./my_strace /nonexistent_binary`  
Expected: child's `execvp` fails, `perror` output from child, `+++ exited with 1 +++` from parent.
### Phase 3: Entry/Exit Toggle + PTRACE_GETREGS + Print (1–2 hours)
Add `tracee_state_t`, implement `handle_syscall_stop` and `print_syscall_result`. Wire into the loop.
Implementation details:
- Initialize `tracee_state_t state = { .in_syscall = 0, .current_syscall_nr = -1 };` on the stack in `run_tracer`.
- On each `SIGTRAP` stop: `state.in_syscall = !state.in_syscall;` then `handle_syscall_stop(child_pid, &state);`.
- In `handle_syscall_stop`: check `state->in_syscall` (already flipped) to decide entry vs exit.
**Checkpoint 3**: `./my_strace /bin/true 2>&1 | head -20`
Expected output structure:
```
syscall(12) = 94...     # brk or similar
syscall(158) = 0        # arch_prctl
syscall(9) = 94...      # mmap
...
+++ exited with 0 +++
```
Syscall numbers will be small integers (0–335). Return values will be 0 or large positive addresses (for mmap/brk). If you see alternating identical lines or `= -1` for everything, the toggle is wrong.
**Verify toggle correctness**: `./my_strace /bin/true 2>&1 | grep "syscall(39)"` — syscall 39 is `getpid`, should return a small positive integer (the child's PID). If it returns a negative value or the PID of a strange process, the toggle is inverted.
### Phase 4: Error Return Detection + errno_name + Signal Re-injection (1–2 hours)
Add `is_error_return`, `errno_name`, and the signal re-injection branch in the loop.
Implementation details:
- In `print_syscall_result`: check `is_error_return(retval)` first; if true, compute `err = (int)(-retval)` and print error format.
- In the `WIFSTOPPED` branch: after checking `stop_sig == SIGTRAP`, add `else` branch that calls `ptrace(PTRACE_SYSCALL, pid, NULL, stop_sig)` and `continue`.
**Checkpoint 4a — Error detection**: `./my_strace cat /nonexistent_file 2>&1 | grep ENOENT`
Expected: at least one line matching:
```
syscall(2) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or directory)
```
(Syscall 2 is `open`; the exact syscall number depends on whether glibc uses `open` or `openat`.)
**Checkpoint 4b — Signal re-injection**: Test with a program that uses signals.
```bash
# Create test program
cat > /tmp/test_signal.c << 'EOF'
#include <stdio.h>
#include <signal.h>
#include <unistd.h>
void handler(int sig) { printf("caught signal %d\n", sig); }
int main() {
    signal(SIGUSR1, handler);
    kill(getpid(), SIGUSR1);
    printf("after signal\n");
    return 0;
}
EOF
gcc -o /tmp/test_signal /tmp/test_signal.c
./my_strace /tmp/test_signal 2>/tmp/trace.log
```
Expected: the program's stdout shows "caught signal 10" and "after signal". If signal re-injection is broken (passing 0 instead of stop_sig), the program will never execute the signal handler and "caught signal" will not appear.
**Checkpoint 4c — SIGINT pass-through**: `./my_strace sleep 100` then press Ctrl+C.
Expected: `sleep` terminates, tracer prints `+++ killed by signal 2 (Interrupt) +++`. If re-injection is broken, pressing Ctrl+C kills only the tracer while `sleep` continues running in the background.

## 9. Performance Targets
{{DIAGRAM:tdd-diag-6}}
| Operation | Target | Measurement Method |
|---|---|---|
| Tracer loop latency per ptrace stop | 2–10 µs | `time ./my_strace /bin/true 2>/dev/null`; divide elapsed time by syscall count |
| `PTRACE_GETREGS` round-trip | ~1 µs (dominated by context switch) | Instrument with `clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC)` around PTRACE_GETREGS call in debug build |
| Observed slowdown vs native | 5–20× for small programs | `time /bin/true` vs `time ./my_strace /bin/true 2>/dev/null` |
| `tracee_state_t` size | ≤ 16 bytes (1 cache line accessible without fetch boundary crossing) | `printf("%zu\n", sizeof(tracee_state_t))` — must be ≤ 16 |
| `errno_name` lookup time | < 50 ns (switch statement, O(1) on modern compilers) | Not measurable in isolation; dominated by surrounding ptrace cost |
| `is_error_return` evaluation | < 2 ns (two comparisons, branchless on modern CPUs) | Not measurable in isolation |
| Peak memory (tracer process) | < 512 KB RSS | `valgrind --tool=massif ./my_strace /bin/true 2>/dev/null` |
{{DIAGRAM:tdd-diag-7}}
**Context on the 5–20× slowdown target**: `/bin/true` makes roughly 20–50 syscalls. Each incurs two ptrace stops (entry + exit), two `waitpid` calls, one `PTRACE_GETREGS` call, and one `PTRACE_SYSCALL` resume — approximately 5 kernel transitions per syscall. At 1–5 µs each, 50 syscalls × 5 transitions × 3 µs = 750 µs overhead on top of a native time of ~1 ms. The observed ratio for `/bin/true` will be in the 2–5× range; for syscall-heavy programs like `ls`, 10–20×.
